verbatim_text: So now let's move on to the hard thing. And just to put it in different words, right, in the simple setting, you know, you have your life experience so far, but Karthik hands you the lecture note. And as long as, you know, you apply yourself and actually learn the lecture stuff, you'll be able to solve, you know, at least a one-star exercise. But in the hard setting, right, Karthik doesn't give you the lecture note or, you know, it's an open research problem that's outside of the boundary of all existing human knowledge and Karthik doesn't even know the answer. In fact, this might be, you know, a definition of research. So how do you approach this? In machine learning, we have an abstraction for this sort of idea. It's called out-of-distribution generalization. And basically that test instance is not represented by the training data. And when I first studied this topic in 2018, you know, the models weren't powerful enough to discover new proofs, so I was working on this abstraction in another application, this object recognition. Okay, so we will be training on ImageNet and then testing on ImageNet on a snowy day. And you wouldn't have access to any snowy day, you know, image during a training, okay? You wouldn't know that it would be snowy. So how do you deal with this problem?
